chunk_summary: Refeeding syndrome in SAM management requires recognition of electrolyte shifts, proactive supplementation, and careful feed advancement to prevent clinical deterioration.
learning_objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of refeeding syndrome in severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
Identify clinical signs and laboratory markers of refeeding syndrome during therapeutic feeding.
Learn appropriate thiamine and phosphate replacement strategies for managing refeeding syndrome.
Develop skills to adjust feeding protocols and manage concurrent infections in SAM patients.
key_facts: Refeeding syndrome risk arises from depleted potassium, magnesium, and phosphate stores, with electrolyte shifts driven by insulin release during feeding.
Clinical signs include tachypnea, hypoxia, arrhythmia, neuromuscular weakness, seizures, or worsening edema within 2–5 days of feed advancement.
Routine supplementation: potassium (3–4 mmol/kg/day) and magnesium (0.4–0.6 mmol/kg/day) even without lab confirmation.
Thiamine supplementation is recommended before escalating feeds in refeeding syndrome cases.
Moderate-to-severe hypophosphatemia requires enteral phosphate or IV phosphate with cardiac and calcium monitoring.
reference_quote: Suspect refeeding syndrome when new tachypnea or hypoxia develops without another explanation, arrhythmia or sudden tachycardia occurs, neuromuscular weakness or lethargy worsens, seizures appear, or edema increases during feed advancement. (Source: WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children, Severe Acute Malnutrition Chapter)
document_key_topics: Clinical signs and prevention of refeeding complications
